---
title: "Creeping Tick Trefoil (Grona triflora)"
slug: "grona-triflora"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180776099/medium.jpg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Creeping Tick Trefoil"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Fabales"
tax_family: "Fabaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Fabales", "Fabaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180776099/medium.jpg" alt="Creeping Tick Trefoil" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/lucakuziel">
lucakuziel</a> (CC BY) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107478841">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Fabales
-   **Family:** Fabaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=1243255)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Common across Florida and found in lawns, pastures, and disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A low-growing, spreading legume characterized by its trifoliate leaves and small pink to purple flowers. In Florida, it is commonly found in sandy, open habitats such as lawns, roadsides, and disturbed areas. It grows primarily during the warmer months and produces segmented seed pods that can stick to fur or clothing, aiding in dispersal.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/1243255-Grona-triflora)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/1495)
